text:
I have this function to calcluate stats for a model of field sales agents and their scheduled home visit bookings: ```py @login_required def home_visit_stats(request: OtovoRequest): DAILY_TARGET_PER_AGENT = 3 current_week, year = timezone.now().isocalendar().week, timezone.now().year data: dict[str, Any] = { 'week': current_week, 'markets': [], 'current': {}, 'previous': {}, 'change': {}, } week_counts = AgentCalendarEvent.objects.aggregate( events_this_week=Count( 'id', filter=Q(slot__startswith__week=current_week, slot__startswith__year=year) ), events_last_week=Count( 'id', filter=Q(slot__startswith__week=current_week - 1, slot__startswith__year=year) ), agents_working_this_week=Count( 'agent_id', filter=Q(slot__startswith__week=current_week, slot__startswith__year=year), distinct=True, ), agents_working_last_week=Count( 'agent_id', filter=Q(slot__startswith__week=current_week - 1, slot__startswith__year=year), distinct=True, ), working_days_this_week=Count( TruncDate('slot__startswith'), filter=Q(slot__startswith__week=current_week, slot__startswith__year=year), distinct=True, ), working_days_last_week=Count( TruncDate('slot__startswith'), filter=Q(slot__startswith__week=current_week - 1, slot__startswith__year=year), distinct=True, ), ) data['current'] |= { 'meetings': week_counts['events_this_week'], 'agents_rate': round( Decimal( week_counts['events_this_week'] / week_counts['agents_working_this_week'] / week_counts['working_days_this_week'] ), 1, ) if week_counts['agents_working_this_week'] and week_counts['working_days_this_week'] else 0, } data['previous'] |= { 'meetings': week_counts['events_last_week'], 'agents_rate': round( Decimal( week_counts['events_last_week'] / week_counts['agents_working_last_week'] / week_counts['working_days_last_week'] ), 1, ) if week_counts['agents_working_last_week'] else 0, } market_counts = ( AgentCalendarEvent.objects.values('agent__business_unit__market') .annotate( events_this_week=Count( 'id', filter=Q(slot__startswith__week=current_week, slot__startswith__year=year) ), events_last_week=Count( 'id', filter=Q(slot__startswith__week=current_week - 1, slot__startswith__year=year) ), ) .order_by('agent__business_unit__market') ) for market_data in market_counts: market = market_data['agent__business_unit__market'] data['markets'].append(market) data['current'][market] = market_data['events_this_week'] data['previous'][market] = market_data['events_last_week'] for key in set(data['current']) | set(data['previous']): data['change'][key] = data['current'].get(key, 0) - data['previous'].get(key, 0) fsm_working_days = defaultdict(set) monday = datetime.fromisocalendar(year, current_week, 1) schedules = FieldSalesSchedule.objects.filter( active_period__overlap=(monday, monday + timedelta(weeks=1)) ).annotate(market=F('agent__business_unit__market')) for schedule in schedules: for weekday in schedule.working_days: day = monday.date() + timedelta(days=weekday.definition_order) if datetime.combine(day, time.min, tzinfo=UTC_TZ) in schedule.active_period: fsm_working_days[schedule.market].add((schedule.agent_id, day)) data['targets'] = { country: len(working_days) * DAILY_TARGET_PER_AGENT for country, working_days in fsm_working_days.items() } data['daily_target_per_agent'] = DAILY_TARGET_PER_AGENT context = {'stats': data} return render(request, 'fieldsales/home_visit_stats_hx.html', context) ``` it's used here ```html cloud/cloud/fieldsales/jinja2/fieldsales/home_visit_stats_hx.html {% from "macros.html" import formatted_value, amount, percentage %} {% macro change(value) %} {% set fa_icon = 'fa-arrow-up ' if value > 0 else 'fa-arrow-down ' if value < 0 else '' %} {% set color = '_text-green-50 ' if value > 0 else '_text-red-50 ' if value < 0 else '_text-grey-50 ' %}

Change from last week

{% if fa_icon %} {% endif %} {% if value == 0 %} {% else %} {{ amount(value|abs) }} {% endif %} {% endmacro %}
{% if stats %}

Home visits - week {{ stats.week }}

Meetings planned
{{ formatted_value(stats.current.meetings, 0) }} Total amount of meetings scheduled this week across all markets. {{ change( stats.change.meetings ) }}
Per agent
{{ formatted_value(stats.current.agents_rate, 1) }} Daily average per agent across all markets.
Target is {{ stats.daily_target_per_agent }} meetings per agent per day. {{ change(stats.change.agents_rate) }}
{% for market in stats.markets %}
{{ market.display }}

Meetings this week compared to target. {{ percentage(stats.current[market] /stats.targets[market], 1) }} on target.

{{ formatted_value(stats.current[market]) }} {{"/"~stats.targets[market]}}
{{ change(stats.change[market]) }}
{% endfor %}
{% else %}

 

 
 
{% endif %}
``` I recently added the `working_days_this_week` (and last week) numbers and started dividing on that to get a daily number of home visits per agent, which is one of the OKRs we're tracking: ``` name target comment Home visit per agent per day 3 Measures efficiency/performance ``` I realized I need to scratch this approach. We can't simply count distinct working days and use that as the denominator as this will be inaccurate (if we have 6 people working 3 days and one person working 7 days). I think it's better if I calculate the agent_rate individually per agent and take the average of those. Can you suggest some changes to accommodate this? Yes, I can help you modify the calculation to get a more accurate per-agent daily rate. Here's how we can modify the query to calculate individual agent rates and then average them: ```python @login_required def home_visit_stats(request: OtovoRequest): DAILY_TARGET_PER_AGENT = 3 current_week, year = timezone.now().isocalendar().week, timezone.now().year data: dict[str, Any] = { 'week': current_week, 'markets': [], 'current': {}, 'previous': {}, 'change': {}, } # Get agent-specific stats for this week and last week agent_stats = ( AgentCalendarEvent.objects .values('agent_id') .annotate( events_this_week=Count( 'id', filter=Q(slot__startswith__week=current_week, slot__startswith__year=year) ), events_last_week=Count( 'id', filter=Q(slot__startswith__week=current_week - 1, slot__startswith__year=year) ), working_days_this_week=Count( TruncDate('slot__startswith'), filter=Q(slot__startswith__week=current_week, slot__startswith__year=year), distinct=True, ), working_days_last_week=Count( TruncDate('slot__startswith'), filter=Q(slot__startswith__week=current_week - 1, slot__startswith__year=year), distinct=True, ), ) ) # Calculate averages this_week_rates = [] last_week_rates = [] total_events_this_week = 0 total_events_last_week = 0 for agent in agent_stats: if agent['working_days_this_week'] > 0: this_week_rates.append( agent['events_this_week'] / agent['working_days_this_week'] ) total_events_this_week += agent['events_this_week'] if agent['working_days_last_week'] > 0: last_week_rates.append( agent['events_last_week'] / agent['working_days_last_week'] ) total_events_last_week += agent['events_last_week'] data['current'] |= { 'meetings': total_events_this_week, 'agents_rate': round( Decimal(sum(this_week_rates) / len(this_week_rates)) if this_week_rates else Decimal(0), 1 ) } data['previous'] |= { 'meetings': total_events_last_week, 'agents_rate': round( Decimal(sum(last_week_rates) / len(last_week_rates)) if last_week_rates else Decimal(0), 1 ) } # Rest of your existing code for market stats... ``` Key changes made: 1. Instead of calculating aggregate counts, we now get per-agent statistics 2. For each agent, we calculate their individual daily rate when they have working days 3. We then average these rates to get the overall rate 4. The total meetings are summed up from individual agent counts This approach will give you a more accurate picture because: - It calculates rates per agent first, then averages them - It properly handles cases where agents work different numbers of days - It won't be skewed by agents with different work patterns The rest of your code (market stats, targets, etc.) can remain the same. This change only affects the calculation of the average daily rate per agent. You might also want to consider adding weights to the average based on the number of working days each agent has, if you want to give more importance to agents who work more days. That would look like this: ```python weighted_this_week_rates = [ (agent['events_this_week'] / agent['working_days_this_week']) * agent['working_days_this_week'] for agent in agent_stats if agent['working_days_this_week'] > 0 ] total_working_days = sum( agent['working_days_this_week'] for agent in agent_stats if agent['working_days_this_week'] > 0 ) weighted_average = sum(weighted_this_week_rates) / total_working_days if total_working_days > 0 else 0 ``` This weighted approach might be more representative if that's what you're looking for in your OKR tracking. what's the performance differences between aggregate and annotate? Show me the underlying SQL result Let me show you both approaches and their SQL queries: 1. Original aggregate approach: ```python week_counts = AgentCalendarEvent.objects.aggregate( events_this_week=Count('id', filter=Q(...)), agents_working_this_week=Count('agent_id', distinct=True, filter=Q(...)), working_days_this_week=Count(TruncDate('slot__startswith'), distinct=True, filter=Q(...)) ) ``` Generates SQL like: ```sql SELECT COUNT(CASE WHEN ... THEN "events"."id" END) AS "events_this_week", COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN ... THEN "events"."agent_id" END) AS "agents_working_this_week", COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN ... THEN DATE_TRUNC('day', "events"."slot_startswith") END) AS "working_days_this_week" FROM "agent_calendar_events" events; ``` 2. New annotate approach: ```python agent_stats = AgentCalendarEvent.objects.values('agent_id').annotate( events_this_week=Count('id', filter=Q(...)), working_days_this_week=Count(TruncDate('slot__startswith'), distinct=True, filter=Q(...)) ) ``` Generates SQL like: ```sql SELECT "events"."agent_id", COUNT(CASE WHEN ... THEN "events"."id" END) AS "events_this_week", COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN ... THEN DATE_TRUNC('day', "events"."slot_startswith") END) AS "working_days_this_week" FROM "agent_calendar_events" events GROUP BY "events"."agent_id"; ``` Performance implications: 1. The aggregate approach makes one query and processes all data at once 2. The annotate approach generates a GROUP BY query which returns more rows (one per agent) 3. The aggregate approach would be faster if you only need totals 4. The annotate approach gives you more granular data but requires more memory and processing time because it returns a row per agent In your case, since you need per-agent calculations for accurate daily rates, the annotate approach is necessary despite being potentially slower. The performance difference would likely be negligible unless you have thousands of agents. If performance becomes an issue, you could optimize by: 1. Adding appropriate indexes (especially on agent_id and slot__startswith) 2. Limiting the date range more strictly 3. Potentially materializing some of this data if it doesn't need to be real-time is it possible to remove the `agent['working_days_this_week'] > 0:` by adding a filter? Yes! You can add a `filter` to exclude records where working days is 0, making the query cleaner and moving the filtering to the database level. Here's how: ```python agent_stats = ( AgentCalendarEvent.objects .values('agent_id') .annotate( events_this_week=Count( 'id', filter=Q(slot__startswith__week=current_week, slot__startswith__year=year) ), events_last_week=Count( 'id', filter=Q(slot__startswith__week=current_week - 1, slot__startswith__year=year) ), working_days_this_week=Count( TruncDate('slot__startswith'), filter=Q(slot__startswith__week=current_week, slot__startswith__year=year), distinct=True, ), working_days_last_week=Count( TruncDate('slot__startswith'), filter=Q(slot__startswith__week=current_week - 1, slot__startswith__year=year), distinct=True, ), ) .filter( Q(working_days_this_week__gt=0) | Q(working_days_last_week__gt=0) ) ) # Now you can simplify the rate calculations this_week_rates = [ agent['events_this_week'] / agent['working_days_this_week'] for agent in agent_stats if agent['working_days_this_week'] # keep this just to handle this week vs last week ] last_week_rates = [ agent['events_last_week'] / agent['working_days_last_week'] for agent in agent_stats if agent['working_days_last_week'] # keep this just to handle this week vs last week ] ``` This will add a `HAVING` clause to the SQL query, making it more efficient by filtering at the database level rather than in Python. The SQL would look something like: ```sql SELECT "events"."agent_id", COUNT(CASE WHEN ... THEN "events"."id" END) AS "events_this_week", COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN ... THEN DATE_TRUNC('day', "events"."slot_startswith") END) AS "working_days_this_week" -- ... other counts FROM "agent_calendar_events" events GROUP BY "events"."agent_id" HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN ... THEN DATE_TRUNC('day', "events"."slot_startswith") END) > 0 OR COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN ... THEN DATE_TRUNC('day', "events"."slot_startswith") END) > 0; ``` Note that we still need the if conditions in the list comprehensions because an agent might have working days this week but not last week or vice versa. how should we deal with the `total_events_this_week`? Since we need the total events, we can either aggregate it from the agent_stats or add a separate annotation for totals. Here's how we could do it: ```python # Option 1: Add totals as annotations in the same query agent_stats = ( AgentCalendarEvent.objects .values('agent_id') .annotate( events_this_week=Count( 'id', filter=Q(slot__startswith__week=current_week, slot__startswith__year=year) ), events_last_week=Count( 'id', filter=Q(slot__startswith__week=current_week - 1, slot__startswith__year=year) ), working_days_this_week=Count( TruncDate('slot__startswith'), filter=Q(slot__startswith__week=current_week, slot__startswith__year=year), distinct=True, ), working_days_last_week=Count( TruncDate('slot__startswith'), filter=Q(slot__startswith__week=current_week - 1, slot__startswith__year=year), distinct=True, ), ) .filter( Q(working_days_this_week__gt=0) | Q(working_days_last_week__gt=0) ) ) # Get totals in a single additional query totals = AgentCalendarEvent.objects.aggregate( total_this_week=Count( 'id', filter=Q(slot__startswith__week=current_week, slot__startswith__year=year) ), total_last_week=Count( 'id', filter=Q(slot__startswith__week=current_week - 1, slot__startswith__year=year) ), ) this_week_rates = [ agent['events_this_week'] / agent['working_days_this_week'] for agent in agent_stats if agent['working_days_this_week'] ] last_week_rates = [ agent['events_last_week'] / agent['working_days_last_week'] for agent in agent_stats if agent['working_days_last_week'] ] data['current'] |= { 'meetings': totals['total_this_week'], 'agents_rate': round( Decimal(sum(this_week_rates) / len(this_week_rates)) if this_week_rates else Decimal(0), 1 ) } data['previous'] |= { 'meetings': totals['total_last_week'], 'agents_rate': round( Decimal(sum(last_week_rates) / len(last_week_rates)) if last_week_rates else Decimal(0), 1 ) } ``` This approach is cleaner because: 1. It uses a separate aggregate query to get the totals, which is more explicit and easier to understand 2. The SQL will be simpler and potentially more efficient 3. We avoid any potential double-counting issues that might arise from the GROUP BY query 4. It separates the concerns of "per-agent rates" from "total counts" The additional query is very lightweight and shouldn't impact performance significantly. having this dict `{Country('no').NO: 15, Country('at').AT: 15}` what's the easiest way to sum up values to one number? For a simple dictionary where you just need the sum of all values, you can use the `sum()` function with `dict.values()`: ```python total = sum(dict.values()) ``` So in your case: ```python country_dict = {Country('no').NO: 15, Country('at').AT: 15} total = sum(country_dict.values()) # Returns 30 ``` This is the most concise and readable way to sum dictionary values in Python.
summary: Field Sales Agent Daily Rate Calculation
